title: Transformation-mediated twinning governs plasticity in body-centered cubic nanocrystals under extreme loading
authors: Jan Očenášek, Jesper Byggmästar, Guanying Wei, F. Javier Dominguez-Gutierrez, Jorge Alcalá
abstract: Plasticity in body-centered cubic (BCC) nanocrystals is often associated with twin nucleation phenomena under extreme loading conditions. Here, we reveal unconventional twinning pathways that operate at the intersection of crystal plasticity and structural phase transitions. We show that the classical shear-driven twinning mode becomes progressively suppressed with increasing pressure, giving rise to transformation-mediated twinning pathways involving transient HCP or FCC phases. In BCC Fe, Ta, and Nb nanocrystals of moderate elastic stiffness, plasticity is consistently initiated by an elastic instability that triggers a dual-shuffle process mediated by stable or metastable hexagonal closed-packed (HCP) phases. This pathway operates independently of the characteristic {112} twin boundary planes and is driven by compression, challenging the conceptual paradigm for metal plasticity in which plastic deformation arises from shear stresses resolved on specific planes. By contrast, in the archetypal elastically stiffer BCC Mo and W nanocrystals, plastic deformation proceeds via two alternative twinning pathways associated with shear-driven elastic instabilities mediated by highly-distorted face-centered cubic (FCC) phases. Comprehensive analyses of the energy landscapes to the competing nanoscale twinning modes provide mechanistic insight into their activation, establishing a unified framework for transformation-mediated twinning in BCC nanocrystals across a broad range of loading conditions.
summary: This paper identifies new, pressure-driven twinning mechanisms in BCC metals that deviate from the classical shear-driven model. It demonstrates that extreme loading can trigger structural phase transitions through transient HCP or FCC phases, providing a unified framework for plasticity in nanocrystals.
main_problem: Understanding the atomic-scale mechanisms of twinning and plasticity in BCC nanocrystals under extreme loading conditions, specifically how high pressure suppresses classical shear-driven twinning in favor of transformation-mediated pathways.
main_result: The study reveals that increasing pressure suppresses classical shear-driven twinning and activates new pathways: a pressure-driven 'dual-shuffle' process involving transient HCP phases in moderately stiff metals (Fe, Ta, Nb) and an FCC-mediated pathway in stiffer metals (W, Mo).
method: The researchers used advanced Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations with quantum-level accurate (tabGAP) and EAM potentials, supplemented by Density Functional Theory (DFT) to analyze energy landscapes and structural transitions.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of BCC metallic nanocrystals, specifically Fe, Ta, Nb, W, and Mo, subjected to extreme loading regimes including uniaxial compression, tension, hydrostatic pressure, and nanoindentation.
key_observables: Twin nucleation/growth, twin boundary morphology (staggered vs. straight), correspondence angles, transient HCP/FCC phases, and energy landscapes (fault energies).
important_parameters: Extreme loading regimes (high pressure/stress), elastic stiffness of the metal, and crystallographic orientations.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the accuracy of the tabGAP and EAM interatomic potentials for capturing phase transitions and relies on the validity of the MD/DFT framework for nanoscale deformation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows classic twinning under tension; Figure 2 details the BCC-HCP-BCC dual-shuffle pathway and correspondence angles; Figure 3 visualizes staggered twin boundaries in Ta; Figure 5 shows FCC-like platelets in W/Mo; Figure 6 presents energy landscapes for different twinning routes; Figure 7 shows stress-strain curves.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of BCC plasticity under extreme loads, presents the discovery of the dual-shuffle and FCC-mediated pathways, compares these mechanisms based on material elastic stiffness, validates the findings using energy landscape analysis and potential comparison, and concludes with a unified framework for BCC twinning.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
